headline: EXPORT SALES REPORT DUE; OATS BREAKDOWN OVERNIGHT
subheadline: Corn and beans hold modest gains ahead of Thursday's 7:30 AM export number, while oats take a 10% hit overnight with no clean catalyst.
lead: The week's most consequential number drops at 7:30 AM CT: USDA weekly export sales, with the trade watching soybean figures especially closely after traders flagged optimism about China demand ahead of the print. Corn is up 2 cents to $4.07 and beans are up 4.5 cents to $11.11, both modest and cautious, the kind of positioning you see when the market doesn't want to be caught leaning the wrong way. The oats board is a different story, down 10.3% overnight with no news driving it, that kind of magnitude without a headline reads like forced liquidation.
the_takeaway: Wait on soybeans until export sales print; the China thesis needs numbers behind it.
teaser: Export sales at 7:30 AM will either put a floor under the grain complex or remove the last bullish argument.

one_number.unit: overnight oats decline, no news driver
one_number.context: Oats fell to $2.86, now sitting at just 10% above their 52-week low, in a single overnight session with nothing in the news bucket to explain it. That kind of magnitude without a catalyst reads as forced liquidation, likely a fund or two unwinding a position that had nowhere to go. It doesn't move corn or beans directly, but it's a yellow flag on grain complex sentiment heading into the export print.

yesterdays_call.summary: Producers with on-farm grain were told to check local basis; if basis was firm, the futures break had narrowed the window, and to evaluate before the heat forecast got priced into new-crop.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn recovered 2 cents today but is still sitting at $4.07 after yesterday's break; basis resolution depends on local conditions the futures board alone can't confirm, and the export number this morning will be the real test.

sections.title: GRAINS HOLD, EXPORT SALES LOOM
sections.body: Corn closed at **$4.065** nearby, December at **$4.35** -- both adding roughly 2 cents on the session in what the tape is calling cautious pre-positioning, not a trend. Soybeans added 4.5 cents to **$11.11** nearby, November up 5.5 cents to **$11.38**. Neither move has conviction behind it. Traders flagged pre-positioning ahead of this morning's USDA weekly export sales, with the trade specifically watching soybean demand from China under the $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment announced May 18. China's purchases have not yet translated into sustained futures support past the initial bounce, and today's 7:30 print is the next test of whether that commitment is moving physical grain. Chicago wheat ticked up less than a cent to **$5.865**, still range-bound and waiting on somebody else to set the direction. With pollination approaching across the Belt, any heat-stress story in next Monday's Crop Progress will add a premium that today's prices are not carrying.
sections.bottom_line: Grains are coiled around the 7:30 print; under 300K MT soybean sales removes the bullish argument and hands the chart back to the funds.
sections.farmer_action: Old-crop soybean holders: if export sales come in above 400K MT, basis is likely to firm. Evaluate sales before the number resets the story.

sections.title: OATS DROP HARD OVERNIGHT
sections.body: Oats fell 32.75 cents to **$2.86**, a 10.3% overnight drop that puts the contract at just 10% above its 52-week low. There is nothing in the news bucket that explains a move of this size. No USDA report, no crop damage announcement, no export cancellation. Looks like forced fund liquidation, a position that had to move and found no bids on the way down. The oats market is thin relative to corn and beans, which means a single large unwinding can print a number that looks like news when it isn't. Watch whether the selling bleeds into the broader grain complex at the open, but the direct read for corn and soybean producers is: this is an oats-specific event, not a signal about your crop.
sections.catalyst: No clean catalyst; magnitude and timing point to forced fund liquidation.
sections.bottom_line: Oats liquidation is oats-specific; watch for morning contagion but don't read it as grain-complex signal.

sections.title: CATTLE FIRM; FEEDER STRENGTH HOLDS
sections.body: Live cattle added 0.2% to **$246.65**, feeder cattle ran 1.3% higher to **$373.23**. The feeder strength is doing real work here: tight ready supplies are supporting the live market even as the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began May 19 keeps processing-side pressure in the background with 1,700 workers still locked out and roughly 2% of weekly US slaughter capacity off-line. A Cattle on Feed report last week flagged worsening household finances and softer beef demand in May, a cautionary note that hasn't fully priced into futures but sits in the back pocket. The live/feeder spread at current prices is still historically wide. Feeder strength today is either telling you the supply tightness is real and durable, or the funds are getting ahead of a direct trade that hasn't printed yet. The direct cash business developing this week will answer that.
sections.catalyst: Tight ready supplies; Cargill lockout continues to constrain processing; Cattle on Feed demand caution.
sections.bottom_line: Feeder strength doing the heavy lifting; wait on cash trade to confirm or deny.

sections.title: ENERGY STEADY; HORMUZ PREMIUM FADES
sections.body: WTI crude added 0.2% to **$69.49**, natural gas firmed 1.5% to **$3.33**. The natural gas move fits: Qatar's gas exporting countries head said this week that natural gas markets are on course to return to balance in Q3 as the Strait of Hormuz reopens. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, continue to give back the premium that was holding crude higher through April and May. Five oil and gas carriers cleared the Strait the week of May 25, and Qatar inked a crude deal with a Taiwanese refiner this week, the latest sign that Gulf oil flows are recovering. For producers, the diesel side of the input equation is getting marginally cheaper each week this premium deflates. Fertilizer anhydrous ammonia pricing has not moved in step yet, but the directional pressure is lower.
sections.catalyst: Qatar LNG output restoration timeline confirmed; Gulf crude trade recovering; Hormuz premium deflating.
sections.bottom_line: Energy inputs trending softer; fuel cost tailwind building slowly for fall operations.

outside_the_pit.title: White House Requests $11.1 Billion in Emergency Farm Aid
outside_the_pit.body: The White House called on Congress Wednesday to pass $11.1 billion in emergency farm aid in a supplemental appropriations bill, with Senate Agriculture Appropriations leaders Hoeven and Boozman moving to work on the request. The number is large enough to matter for producers still carrying losses from the wet 2026 planting start and storm damage across the Belt.

outside_the_pit.title: EPA Finalizes RFS 'Set 2' Rule for 2026-2027 Biomass Diesel
outside_the_pit.body: EPA finalized the 'Set 2' Renewable Volume Obligations under the RFS for 2026 and 2027, locking in the biofuel blending mandate that underpins soybean oil demand. Farmdoc analysis this week breaks down what the RVOs mean for biomass-based diesel feedstock projections; soybean oil at $65.40 today is partly anchored by this mandate floor.

outside_the_pit.title: Crop Insurance Guidance Critical as Storm Damage Mounts Across Belt
outside_the_pit.body: A southwest Iowa crop insurance agent highlighted this week that producers dealing with storm damage need to document losses carefully before any field work to protect policy claims. With soil moisture running 150% of normal in key districts and storm systems continuing to track through the Belt, the insurance decision window is tighter than it looks.

spread_to_watch.label: November beans / nearby beans carry
spread_to_watch.level: $0.27 carry, new crop over old crop, steady
spread_to_watch.commentary: November beans at $11.38 over nearby at $11.11 is a 27-cent carry that says the market isn't panicking about old-crop supply but isn't offering a premium to store either. If today's export sales print strong on the nearby contract and old-crop basis firms, watch whether that carry compresses; a narrowing spread would tell you merchandisers are pulling old crop out of the pipeline faster than expected.

basis.headline: Corn basis mixed; bean basis firm ahead of export print.
basis.body: **Corn basis is mixed across the Belt** following the nearby contract's 6% break earlier this week. Eastern Belt locations holding firm basis have a narrowing window as the futures discount catches up to local prices. Western Belt basis softening in line with seasonal patterns. **Soybean basis is firming at interior elevators** ahead of Thursday's export number, with merchandisers positioning for a potential China demand confirmation. Producers with old-crop beans still in storage should check local basis this morning before the 7:30 CT number resets the conversation.

weekly_thread.question: Does USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt is ahead of pace, removing the last weather premium from corn?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's Crop Progress confirmed corn at 42% planted as of May 24, ahead of the 5-year average with no weather premium being priced. This week's focus has shifted: the question now is whether pollination-period heat stress adds a premium back before the next Monday report. Thursday's export sales won't answer the crop condition question directly, but a strong China soybean number would tell you demand is leaning in regardless of what the weather does to yield.

the_more_you_know.title: Pollination Premium: The Weather Risk the Market Hasn't Priced Yet
the_more_you_know.body: Corn at $4.07 is sitting 33% above its 52-week low but carrying no explicit weather premium heading into pollination season. That's the window between now and late July when a stretch of 95-plus degree days with limited overnight cooling can cut yield potential faster than any planting delay. The 2012 drought showed that once pollination stress is visible in the crop condition data, the market moves first and asks questions later. The 2026 Belt entered the season with soil moisture running 150% of normal in key districts, which provides a buffer, but excess moisture that delayed early planting doesn't inoculate against July heat. Monday's Crop Progress report is the first weekly read on crop condition ratings for the 2026 corn crop; if the good-to-excellent percentage comes in below 65%, traders will start building in the premium that today's $4.07 isn't pricing.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybean sales above 400K MT would confirm the China demand thesis and support nearby beans above $11.11; below 300K MT hands the chart back to the funds.
USDA Crop Progress: first corn crop condition ratings of 2026; good-to-excellent below 65% starts pricing a pollination premium the market hasn't built yet.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout: any resolution announcement moves live cattle and feeders; no resolution reported as of this morning.

Farm bill supplemental: White House requested $11.1 billion in farm aid; Senate Ag Appropriations moving; watch for committee markup timeline that would set reference prices affecting crop insurance planning.

daily_quote.text: Nobody ever went broke taking a profit.

source_summary: USDA NASS, CME Group, Brownfield Ag News, Feedstuffs, OilPrice.com, EIA Weekly Petroleum Report, FarmDoc Daily, Beef Magazine, The Fence Post
